Matisse 0.1.0

Getting started with Matisse

Source: vignettes/intro.Rmd
intro.Rmd

What does Matisse do?

Genes can be spliced in different ways: certain exons may be included in some transcripts but skipped in others. This is called alternative splicing, and it means one gene can produce multiple protein isoforms with different functions.

Matisse measures alternative splicing one cell at a time. For each cell in your dataset, it calculates a PSI value (Percent Spliced In) for each splicing event you care about:

  • PSI = 1 — every transcript in that cell includes the exon
  • PSI = 0 — every transcript skips the exon
  • PSI = 0.5 — half include, half skip

By comparing PSI values across your cell types, clusters, or conditions, you can ask which cell populations splice genes differently — and by how much.

Matisse slots directly into your existing Seurat workflow. Your gene expression data, UMAP, and cluster assignments are kept intact; splicing information is simply added alongside them.

Installation 

# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("avisrilab/Matisse")

What data do I need?

Matisse supports two starting points depending on how your data were processed:

My data What I have Path to use
10x short-read (aligned with STAR) Junction counts + Seurat object CreateMatisseObject()
Long-read or Bagpiper transcript counts Transcript counts + Seurat object CreateMatisseObjectFromTranscripts()

Both paths produce the same kind of Matisse object, and all downstream steps (QC, filtering, visualization) are identical.

Path 1: Short-read data (junction counts)

Step 1 — Prepare your data

You need three things:

  1. A Seurat object — your normal single-cell gene expression object, already processed (normalized, clustered, UMAP computed)
  2. A junction count table — records how many reads span each exon-exon junction in each cell; produced by STARsolo alongside your gene counts
  3. A splice event table — defines which junctions belong to which splicing event; generated by tools like rMATS, SUPPA2, or the built-in BuildSimpleEvents() helper
library(Matisse)
library(Seurat)
library(Matrix)

# --- Toy data for illustration ------------------------------------------------
set.seed(42)
n_cells <- 200; n_genes <- 500; n_jxns <- 50

# Simulate a gene expression matrix and process it with Seurat
gene_counts <- matrix(rpois(n_genes * n_cells, 10),
                      nrow     = n_genes,
                      dimnames = list(paste0("Gene", seq_len(n_genes)),
                                      paste0("CELL",  seq_len(n_cells))))

seu <- CreateSeuratObject(gene_counts) |>
  NormalizeData()        |>
  FindVariableFeatures() |>
  ScaleData()            |>
  RunPCA()               |>
  RunUMAP(dims = 1:10)   |>
  FindNeighbors(dims = 1:10) |>
  FindClusters()

# Simulate junction counts (one row per cell, one column per junction)
jxn_counts <- Matrix(
  matrix(rpois(n_cells * n_jxns, lambda = 3),
         nrow     = n_cells,
         dimnames = list(
           colnames(seu),
           paste0("chr1:", seq(1000, by = 500, length.out = n_jxns),
                  "-",     seq(1500, by = 500, length.out = n_jxns), ":+")
         )),
  sparse = TRUE
)

Step 2 — Define splice events

A splice event says: “for this exon, these junctions support inclusion and these support skipping.” If you don’t have a pre-built event table from rMATS or SUPPA2, the BuildSimpleEvents() helper creates a simple one automatically from your junction list.

# Junction annotation table (one row per junction)
jd <- data.frame(
  junction_id = colnames(jxn_counts),
  chr         = "chr1",
  start       = seq(1000, by = 500, length.out = n_jxns),
  end         = seq(1500, by = 500, length.out = n_jxns),
  strand      = "+",
  gene_id     = rep(paste0("Gene", 1:10), each = 5)
)

# Build events: each junction treated as one event vs. all others in the gene
ed <- BuildSimpleEvents(jd)
head(ed)

Step 3 — Create a Matisse object

This combines your Seurat object, junction counts, and event definitions into one place. Your gene expression data and cluster assignments are preserved exactly as they were.

obj <- CreateMatisseObject(
  seurat          = seu,
  junction_counts = jxn_counts,
  event_data      = ed,
  junction_data   = jd
)
obj

Step 4 — Calculate PSI

For each cell and each splicing event, Matisse counts the inclusion and exclusion reads and computes the PSI ratio. Cells with fewer than min_coverage total reads for an event are marked as missing (not enough data to call a reliable splicing ratio).

obj <- CalculatePSI(obj, min_coverage = 5)

Path 2: Transcript counts (Bagpiper / long-read)

If you have isoform-level counts (e.g. from Bagpiper with GENCODE transcript IDs), provide your count table and SUPPA2 .ioe annotation files. Matisse derives the PSI values from transcript abundance rather than junction reads.

# transcript_counts: rows = GENCODE transcript IDs, columns = cell barcodes
obj <- CreateMatisseObjectFromTranscripts(
  seurat            = seu,
  transcript_counts = transcript_counts,
  ioe_files         = c("events_SE.ioe", "events_RI.ioe"),
  min_coverage      = 5L
)

All steps below apply equally to both paths.

Quality control

Before analysing splicing patterns, remove cells and events that don’t have enough data to be reliable.

Calculate QC metrics

This adds per-cell summary statistics to your object: how many junctions were detected, how many splicing events had enough reads, and the average PSI across all covered events.

obj <- ComputeIsoformQC(obj)

# Visualise the QC metrics split by cluster
PlotQCMetrics(obj, group_by = "seurat_clusters")

Remove low-quality cells

Keep only cells that have enough splicing data to be informative:

obj <- FilterCells(
  obj,
  min_junctions      = 5,   # at least 5 junctions detected
  min_junction_reads = 20,  # at least 20 total junction reads
  min_pct_covered    = 10   # PSI measurable for at least 10% of events
)

Remove poorly covered events

Remove splicing events that were measurable in too few cells or that show no variation across the dataset (everyone splices the same way — not interesting):

obj <- FilterEvents(
  obj,
  min_cells_covered = 20,    # event must be measurable in at least 20 cells
  min_psi_variance  = 0.01   # event must show some variation across cells
)

Visualisation

Overlay splicing on your UMAP

Colour each cell by its PSI value for a specific event. Cells with insufficient coverage are shown in grey.

PlotPSIUMAP(obj, event_id = "Gene1:chr1:1000-1500:+:chr1:1000-1500:+")

Compare splicing across cell types 

PlotPSIViolin(
  obj,
  event_id = "Gene1:chr1:1000-1500:+:chr1:1000-1500:+",
  group_by = "seurat_clusters"
)

Survey many events at once 

# Heatmap of PSI values across cells and events
PlotPSIHeatmap(obj, max_cells = 100)

Inspect junction usage for a gene 

# Bar chart of total reads per junction for a gene of interest
PlotJunctionCoverage(obj, gene = "Gene1")

Accessing your data

The Seurat object and all its metadata remain fully accessible at any time:

# Get the Seurat object back
seu_embedded <- GetSeurat(obj)

# Access cell metadata (e.g. cluster assignments) with $
obj$seurat_clusters

# Subset to specific cells — both gene expression and splicing data update together
subset_obj <- obj[c("CELL1", "CELL2", "CELL3"), ]

Session info 

sessionInfo()
#> R version 4.5.3 (2026-03-11)
#> Platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu
#> Running under: Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS
#> 
#> Matrix products: default
#> BLAS:   /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/libblas.so.3 
#> LAPACK: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/openblas-pthread/libopenblasp-r0.3.26.so;  LAPACK version 3.12.0
